content.claim: Between-school ethnic (Roma) segregation in Hungary strengthened substantially between 1980 and 2011; towns with higher inter-school choice commuting display stronger inter-school inequalities; attending a high-poverty or extreme-poverty primary school is associated with lower reading scores, lower secondary completion rates, and lower likelihood of obtaining secondary school-leaving certificate.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Equity and social mobility lens
limitations: Segregation literature is separately well-established but causal link from 6/8-year gimnázium admission to ethnic segregation is inferential/compositional, not measured directly in single study
Kertesi/Kézdi find data noise prevents firm conclusions on 2006–2011 trends
Hermann/Kisfalusi findings on poverty schools do not isolate the specific role of age-10/12 gimnázium selection versus other segregation mechanisms
Interaction effects between academic tracking and ethnic segregation not fully quantified
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
